paper_title: Unprecedented combination of specific tensile strength and ductility achieved in a novel duplex low-density steel

caption: Fig. 1. Microstructures formed in the developed duplex steel after the annealing of the CR structure at $6 0 0 { - } 1 0 5 0 \ { ^ \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 5 min, examined by XRD (a) and TEM (b, c). Cr C carbides were identified in DP950–5 (b), and the intragranular κ-carbide nanoparticles were identified in DP1000–5 (c).
paper_abstract: a State Key Laboratory for Advanced Metallurgy, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Xue Yuan Lu 30, Beijing, 100083, China b Department of Ferrous Metallurgy, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Xue Yuan Lu 30, Beijing, 100083, China c Henan Iron and Steel Group Co., Ltd, Zhengzhou, Henan, 450046, China
paper_title: Unprecedented combination of specific tensile strength and ductility achieved in a novel duplex low-density steel
citation_sentences: XRD results in Fig. 1a indicate that both $\mathrm { { C r } _ { 7 } \mathrm { { C } _ { 3 } } }$ carbides and κ-carbides should be formed during the annealing at 950–1000 ◦C for 5 min. We observed two types of $\mathrm { C r } _ { 7 } \mathrm { C } _ { 3 }$ carbide particles: one has the coarse size of several micrometers and is located in ferrite bands (see Fig. S2a), the other has the size of hundreds of nanometers and precipitates at grain boundaries, see Fig. 1b. κ-carbides can be formed at grain boundaries and the BCC/FCC interfaces with the relatively coarse size of 2–3 μm in DP950–5, see Fig. S2a, or within austenite grains with the ultrafine size $\cot 0 . 8 6 \pm 0 . 2 0$ nm in DP1000–5 (see Fig. 1c). The former usually leads to a brittle fracture whilst the latter formed during water quenching contributes to precipitation strengthening without significantly sacrificing plasticity [1]. When the annealing temperature increases to $1 0 0 0 \ { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ both Cr C carbides and the intergranular κ-carbides are significantly dissolved (see Fig. 1c and Fig. S2b).

The annealing at $9 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 5 min leads to partial recrystallization in austenite with the Rexed fraction of 54.8 % and the ferrite fraction up to 36 % (as determined by EBSD in Fig. 2, i.e., the corresponding δ ferrite bands are thickest, and the granular α ferrite grains are coarsest in DP950–5. This suggests austenite-to-ferrite transformation may occur during the heating to $9 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 5 min, and ferrite-to-austenite transformation may occur above this temperature, both are consistent with the XRD results in Fig. 1a. Meanwhile, $\mathrm { C r } _ { 7 } \mathrm { C } _ { 3 }$ carbides and the intergranular κ-carbides are also formed during the annealing at $9 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (see Fig. 1b and Fig. S2a), i.e., austenite → ferrite $+ \ \mathrm { C r } _ { 7 } \mathrm { C } _ { 3 }$ carbides + the intergranular κ-carbides. Nevertheless, the increase of the annealing temperature from $9 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ leads to thinner δ ferrite bands and finer granular α grains with lower fraction of ferrite (see $\mathrm { F i g . ~ 2 }$ and Table 1). Meanwhile, $\mathrm { C r } _ { 7 } \mathrm { C } _ { 3 }$ carbides and the intergranular κ-carbides start to dissolve significantly at 1000–1050 ◦C.

caption: Fig. 2. EBSD results on the microstructures formed in the developed duplex steel after the different annealing processes. $\left( \mathbf { a } _ { 1 } \mathbf { - } \mathbf { f } _ { 1 } \right)$ phase (PH) mappings and $\left( \mathbf { a } _ { 2 } \mathbf { - } \mathbf { f } _ { 2 } \right)$ grain-reference-orientation-deviation (GROD) mappings of austenite, where dark lines in austenite are high-angle grain boundaries with the misorientation more than 15◦. TD, RD, and ND represent the transverse, rolling, and normal direction, respectively.

paper_title: Unprecedented combination of specific tensile strength and ductility achieved in a novel duplex low-density steel
citation_sentences: The annealing at $9 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 5 min leads to partial recrystallization in austenite with the Rexed fraction of 54.8 % and the ferrite fraction up to 36 % (as determined by EBSD in Fig. 2, i.e., the corresponding δ ferrite bands are thickest, and the granular α ferrite grains are coarsest in DP950–5. This suggests austenite-to-ferrite transformation may occur during the heating to $9 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 5 min, and ferrite-to-austenite transformation may occur above this temperature, both are consistent with the XRD results in Fig. 1a. Meanwhile, $\mathrm { C r } _ { 7 } \mathrm { C } _ { 3 }$ carbides and the intergranular κ-carbides are also formed during the annealing at $9 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (see Fig. 1b and Fig. S2a), i.e., austenite → ferrite $+ \ \mathrm { C r } _ { 7 } \mathrm { C } _ { 3 }$ carbides + the intergranular κ-carbides. Nevertheless, the increase of the annealing temperature from $9 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ to $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ leads to thinner δ ferrite bands and finer granular α grains with lower fraction of ferrite (see $\mathrm { F i g . ~ 2 }$ and Table 1). Meanwhile, $\mathrm { C r } _ { 7 } \mathrm { C } _ { 3 }$ carbides and the intergranular κ-carbides start to dissolve significantly at 1000–1050 ◦C.

The annealing at $1 0 0 0 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ or above also results in a complete recrystallization and the subsequent grain growth, as seen in Fig. 2d-f and Table 1. The Rexed austenite grain size increases from $5 . 1 3 \pm 4 . 7 4$ μm in DP1000–1 to $7 . 4 0 \pm 5 . 1 3$ μm in DP1000–5 and $9 . 6 5 \pm 8 . 1 7$ μm in DP1050–1, because the number and fraction of granular α ferrite both decrease with the increases in the annealing temperature and period, and the presence of the granular α ferrite grains at the boundaries and triple points can effectively retard the austenite grain growth. Recrystallization wipes off the deformed structure such as shear bands and grain growth should significantly reduce the quantity of (sub)grain boundaries so that the nucleation sites for the granular α ferrite grains decrease prominently; consequently, the number and fraction of granular α both decrease more than those of δ bands. In summary, the annealing at $9 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ produces the coarsest δ and α grains (Fig. 2c1), which then partially transform back to austenite when the annealing temperature is further raised.

caption: Fig. 3. Engineering stress-strain curves of our designed duplex lightweight steel subjected to the different annealing processes (a), and comparison of tensile properties of our developed lightweight steel with other existing high-strength metallic materials (b), where the contour lines indicate the product of SUTS and TE ranged from 2 to $8 ~ \mathrm { G P a \cdot g ^ { - 1 } \cdot c m ^ { 3 } } .$ . SUTS and TE represent specific ultimate tensile strength and total elongation, respectively.
paper_abstract: a State Key Laboratory for Advanced Metallurgy, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Xue Yuan Lu 30, Beijing, 100083, China b Department of Ferrous Metallurgy, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Xue Yuan Lu 30, Beijing, 100083, China c Henan Iron and Steel Group Co., Ltd, Zhengzhou, Henan, 450046, China
paper_title: Unprecedented combination of specific tensile strength and ductility achieved in a novel duplex low-density steel
citation_sentences: Both DP1000–5 and DP1050–1 specimens exhibit clearly superior mechanical combination to the existing high-strength metallic materials, see Fig. 3b, because their products of SUTS and TE are more than 8 $\mathbf { G P a { \cdot } g ^ { - 1 } { \cdot } c m ^ { 3 } { \cdot } \% ; }$ whilst less than $4 ~ \mathrm { G P a \cdot g ^ { - 1 } \cdot c m ^ { 3 } \cdot \% }$ for 2000 series aerospace aluminum alloys [34] and titanium alloy of Ti6Al4V [35], and in the ranges of $2 . 7 \mathrm { - } 6 . 5$ and $2 . 9 { - } 7 . 4 ~ \mathrm { G P a \cdot g ^ { - 1 } \cdot c m ^ { 3 } \cdot \% }$ for the duplex [12,13, $^ { 1 6 , 1 9 , 2 0 ] }$ and austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steels [15,36], respectively. In other words, the designed duplex steel has higher specific tensile strength than the Al alloys and other low-density steels at the similar level of ductility, and much more ductile than Ti6Al4V alloy whilst its SUTS is just slightly lower.

The tensile properties of DP1000–5 were also examined along the TD (the resultant specimen termed DP1000–5-TD), and the results are given in Fig. 3a and Table 1. It has the similar YS/UTS but lower TE when compared to DP1000–5. Since only δ ferrite bands have the clear orientation among all the microstructural constituents, they are most likely the reason for lower ductility measured along the TD. In Fig. 4a, a tensile crack in DP1000–5 was supposed to be nucleated at the $\alpha / \gamma$ interface and then propagate along the normal direction (ND) until it encountered a δ ferrite band parallel to the RD, where it was deflected to propagate along the RD. These RD cracks may propagate into the soft δ ferrite bands and then get blunt through dislocation emission and local plastic dissipation in vicinity of the crack tip [37], which significantly delocalized the plastic strain [38]. Therefore, the presence of more δ ferrite bands increases the frequency of capturing and deflecting the ND cracks, the latter are the main cause of tensile failure since thickness is the smallest dimension according to the gauge geometry of tensile specimens. In DP1000–5-TD, however, the $\delta / \gamma$ interfaces are perpendicular to the tensile direction (see Fig. 4b) and subjected to the high de-cohesive stress; consequently, cracks were initiated at these $\delta / \gamma$ interfaces in addition to the $\alpha / \gamma$ interfaces, and then propagated until they quickly got connected across the full thickness during straining, leading to poor ductility.

caption: Fig. 4. SEM and EBSD images on the typical cracks in DP1000–5 (a) and DP1000–5-TD (b). FCC and BCC are austenite and ferrite, respectively. TD, RD, and ND represent the transverse, rolling, and normal direction, respectively.

citation_sentences: The tensile properties of DP1000–5 were also examined along the TD (the resultant specimen termed DP1000–5-TD), and the results are given in Fig. 3a and Table 1. It has the similar YS/UTS but lower TE when compared to DP1000–5. Since only δ ferrite bands have the clear orientation among all the microstructural constituents, they are most likely the reason for lower ductility measured along the TD. In Fig. 4a, a tensile crack in DP1000–5 was supposed to be nucleated at the $\alpha / \gamma$ interface and then propagate along the normal direction (ND) until it encountered a δ ferrite band parallel to the RD, where it was deflected to propagate along the RD. These RD cracks may propagate into the soft δ ferrite bands and then get blunt through dislocation emission and local plastic dissipation in vicinity of the crack tip [37], which significantly delocalized the plastic strain [38]. Therefore, the presence of more δ ferrite bands increases the frequency of capturing and deflecting the ND cracks, the latter are the main cause of tensile failure since thickness is the smallest dimension according to the gauge geometry of tensile specimens.
